sound_type: loud screaming call -- a rare, terrifying, almost-human scream from the deep forest, piercing and sustained, the 67th acoustic element, the loudest mammalian vocalization in the engine but almost never heard, power without prevalence
ecosystem: dense forest -- closed-canopy conifer and mixed forest with structural complexity, large trees for denning, downed logs, snags, >60% canopy closure, the deep forest interior
season: year-round resident in Washington's Olympic and Cascade ranges (reintroduced populations)

coordinates.description: Olympic National Forest reference; reintroduced populations established in the Olympic Peninsula (2008-2010) and South Cascades (2015-2020). Fishers inhabit dense, closed-canopy forest with structural complexity. Vocalizations extremely rare; most likely heard during breeding season disputes in remote montane forest.

taxonomy_note.description: Seventh mammalian entry. Fifth mammalian species. Fourth Carnivora, fourth Mustelidae. FOURTH CONSECUTIVE MUSTELIDAE -- the Mustelidae QUARTET (Otter 63, Mink 64, Marten 65, Fisher 66). Formerly Martes pennanti, reclassified to Pekania pennanti based on molecular phylogenetics -- the Fisher diverged from true martens ~8-12 million years ago. Third reclassification in the quartet (Otter, Mink, Fisher). Monotypic genus.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Pekania from Abenaki (Algonquian) pekan, a Native American word for the Fisher -- one of the few mammalian genera named from a Native American language. pennanti honoring Thomas Pennant (1726-1798), Welsh naturalist who described the species in History of Quadrupeds (1781). Common name 'Fisher' is a misnomer -- Fishers do NOT fish. Possibly from French fiche (polecat pelt) or related to European polecat terminology.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Pekania -- monotypic genus. The Fisher is the sole living member. Erected when molecular phylogenetics demonstrated that the Fisher is NOT a true marten despite two centuries of classification as Martes pennanti. Diverged from the marten lineage approximately 8-12 million years ago.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Carnivora -- fourth entry. All four are Mustelidae (Otter 63, Mink 64, Marten 65, Fisher 66). The order's entire representation in the engine comes from a single family. Carnivora quadruples across the Mustelidae quartet.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- seventh mammalian entry. Fifth mammalian species. Mammalian representation rises to 10.4% (7 of 67 entries). The mammalian era (degrees 61-66) is now six degrees deep. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), three families (Castoridae, Mustelidae).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Mustelidae's fourth entry at degree 66. The QUARTET: Otter (63), Mink (64), Marten (65), Fisher (66). Four consecutive entries -- the engine's deepest family exploration. Mustelidae now represents 4 of 8 PNW mustelid species (50% coverage). Half of Washington's mustelid diversity documented.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Variable, poorly documented. The scream spans a broad frequency range, estimated 500-5,000 Hz with energy concentrated in the 1,000-3,000 Hz band that overlaps with human distress vocalizations.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,000-3,000 (the band most disturbing to human listeners)
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song but a SCREAM. A loud, piercing, sustained vocalization produced during territorial disputes, breeding encounters, and aggressive interactions. Additional vocalizations: growling/hissing (close-range aggression), clucking/chuckling (mother-kit contact), chittering (similar to but deeper than Mink's chitter). The scream dominates -- the sound that defines the species.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual screams: 1-5 seconds. Total vocal event may include multiple screams over 10-30 seconds. But vocal events are EXTREMELY RARE -- a Fisher may go months without screaming.
song_metrics.note_structure: SUSTAINED SCREAM: Unlike the Mink's rapid staccato chitter or the Marten's high screech, the Fisher's scream is a sustained, piercing note -- held rather than pulsed. The sustained quality is what makes it sound human. Some harmonic complexity but not melodic.
song_metrics.note_types: Scream (loud, sustained, territorial/aggressive -- dominant and diagnostic), growl (low, sustained, close-range aggression), hiss (exhalation threat), cluck/chuckle (low-intensity contact), chitter (similar to Mink's but deeper, less prominent). The scream is the Fisher's signature.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single screams or short series (2-4 screams). No phrase variation, no call-and-response documented. The Fisher screams and then silence returns. The isolation of the scream in otherwise silent forest amplifies its impact.
song_metrics.timbre: Harsh, piercing, almost-human. The frequency content and temporal pattern resemble a human scream sufficiently to trigger alarm responses in human listeners. Higher-pitched and more piercing than the Marten's screech, with a sustained quality the Marten lacks.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- LOUD. The loudest mammalian vocalization in the engine. Audible at several hundred meters through dense forest. But heard almost never. The E=5 rating reflects the acoustic power of the scream when produced, not the frequency of production. Power without prevalence.
song_metrics.tempo: Slow for a mustelid vocalization. Where the Mink's chitter is rapid (4-8 notes/second), the Fisher's scream is sustained and deliberate. No rapid pulsing. The scream is held, not hammered.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL/AGGRESSIVE. Produced during breeding season disputes, territorial encounters, and aggressive interactions. NOT social communication. NOT common. A rare vocalization from a rare, secretive animal in remote forest. The acoustic event is notable precisely because it breaks the deep silence of the closed-canopy forest.
song_metrics.regional_variation: NO documented regional variation in vocalizations. Population data insufficient for comparison given recent reintroduction and low densities.
song_metrics.key_property: THE SCREAM -- the 67th acoustic element. Power without prevalence. E=5 but almost never heard. A loud, piercing, almost-human scream from deep forest. The engine's rarest acoustic element. The opposite of the Mallard's ubiquitous E=5 quack. When heard, it is unforgettable and deeply unsettling.

physical_description.overall: Large forest mustelid, 75-120 cm total length, 2-6 kg. The largest terrestrial mustelid in the quartet. Extreme sexual dimorphism -- males nearly twice the weight of females. More robust than the Marten: broader shoulders, thicker neck, more muscular. Built for power and leverage (required for porcupine predation).
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 66, the diagnostic features are: (1) LARGE SIZE -- substantially bigger than Marten, smaller than Otter. (2) DARK FUR WITH FROSTED APPEARANCE -- dark brown to black body with grizzled gold-to-silver sheen on head, neck, and shoulders. (3) BROAD FACE -- wider, more bear-like than the Marten's fox-like face. (4) LARGE FURRED FEET -- no webbing, adapted for forest substrates and snow. (5) BUSHY TAIL -- longer and more prominent than the Marten's.
physical_description.fur_detail: Long, coarse, glossy guard hairs over dense insulating underfur. Body fur dark brown to nearly black, darkest on legs and tail. Head, neck, and shoulders show diagnostic grizzled gold-to-silver 'frosting' -- lighter tips on dark hairs creating a shimmering appearance. No white chin patch (unlike Mink). Wild coloration variable but consistently dark with the frosted pattern.
physical_description.dental_detail: Carnassial teeth as in all Carnivora. Strong jaw muscles for powerful bite. 38 teeth total. The jaw structure and musculature are more robust than the Marten's, reflecting the Fisher's larger prey base and its ability to attack heavily defended prey (porcupines). Kills with neck/skull bite -- the mustelid killing technique scaled up.
physical_description.field_id: In dense forest: a dark, cat-to-small-dog-sized animal with a long, sinuous body, bushy tail, and distinctive frosted shoulders. Larger and darker than the Marten. Moves with a bounding gait on the forest floor. Most active at night and dawn/dusk. Rarely seen -- Fishers are secretive, low-density, and inhabit remote dense forest. Track evidence (large mustelid prints with 5 toes, no claw marks retracted) more commonly encountered than the animal itself. In Washington, only found in the Olympics and Cascades where reintroduced.

nodes.title: The Mustelidae Quartet -- Four Consecutive (63-64-65-66)
nodes.description: Four consecutive Mustelidae: Otter (river/estuary, 5-14 kg, social) -> Mink (creek bank, 0.5-1.5 kg, solitary) -> Marten (old-growth canopy, 0.5-1.2 kg, arboreal) -> Fisher (dense forest, 2-6 kg, terrestrial/arboreal). The engine's deepest family exploration. Four species, four habitats, four sizes, four acoustic elements. A complete habitat gradient from water to deep forest interior within a single family.

nodes.title: The Scream -- 67th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Loud, piercing, almost-human scream. E=5. The loudest mammalian vocalization in the engine but almost never heard. Power without prevalence -- a new acoustic category. The Mallard (E=5) quacks everywhere constantly; the Fisher (E=5) screams almost never in the deepest forest. The engine's rarest acoustic element. Terrifying when heard -- frequency and cadence trigger primate alarm responses.

nodes.title: E=5 and Rarity -- Power Without Prevalence
nodes.description: Second E=5 species-side entry (after Mallard at degree 59). But the Mallard's quack is the most ubiquitous sound in the engine -- heard constantly at every pond. The Fisher's scream is the rarest -- almost never heard, from the deepest forest, at low population density. Maximum acoustic power, minimum frequency of occurrence. This paradox introduces a new dimension to the energy scale: E measures power when produced, not how often it is produced.

nodes.title: First Reintroduced Species -- Extirpated and Returned
nodes.description: The Fisher was completely extirpated from Washington State by trapping and habitat loss. WDFW released Fishers from British Columbia: Olympic Peninsula (2008-2010, 90 animals), South Cascades (2015-2020, ~80 animals), North Cascades (2018-2020). Breeding confirmed in both populations. The engine's first species that exists in its geography only because humans chose to bring it back. No previous species in 66 entries required managed return.

nodes.title: Porcupine Predator -- Specialized Killing Technique
nodes.description: One of the few predators that regularly kills North American Porcupines. Technique: attack the face (only unquilled surface), circling and darting for 25-30 minutes, then flip the exhausted porcupine to expose unprotected belly. Quill-tolerant -- embedded quills encyst rather than causing fatal infection. Behavior appears learned, possibly culturally transmitted from mother to kits. The engine's first documentation of specialized predation technique (vs. surplus killing at degree 64).

nodes.title: Trophic Cascade: Fisher -> Porcupine -> Forest Health
nodes.description: Porcupines girdle trees by feeding on bark and cambium, causing tree mortality. Where Fishers are present, porcupine populations decline and tree damage decreases. Fisher -> porcupine -> tree damage is a documented trophic cascade in North American forests. The Fisher is an indirect benefit to forest health. This is one of the ecological justifications for Fisher reintroduction programs.

nodes.title: Dense Forest Habitat -- The Closed-Canopy Specialist
nodes.description: Dense, closed-canopy forest with >60% canopy closure, large trees (>50 cm DBH) for denning, structural complexity (downed logs, root wads, snags). Not old-growth specifically -- dense younger forest with adequate structure can support Fishers. The Marten (degree 65) needs ANCIENT forest; the Fisher needs DENSE forest. Both need cover, both lose to fragmentation, but the Fisher has slightly broader habitat tolerance. The Fisher completes the quartet's habitat gradient: water -> margin -> canopy -> deep forest floor.

nodes.title: Water-to-Forest Gradient Across the Quartet
nodes.description: Otter (63): river/estuary, aquatic. Mink (64): creek bank, semi-aquatic margin. Marten (65): old-growth canopy, forest/arboreal. Fisher (66): dense forest, ground/midstory. Each transition shifts away from water and into forest interior. The quartet traces a complete ecological gradient from aquatic center to forest interior. The Fisher is the deepest terrestrial point -- defined by distance from water, not proximity to it.

nodes.title: Marten-Fisher Distinction -- Consecutive Forest Mustelids (65-66)
nodes.description: Consecutive Mustelidae, both forest predators, dramatically different in scale. Marten: 0.5-1.2 kg, old-growth canopy specialist, high canopy. Fisher: 2-6 kg, dense forest generalist, ground to midstory. Fisher is 2-3x larger than Marten. The Marten is the canopy expert; the Fisher is the ground-level power predator with canopy access. Same general body plan scaled differently for different ecological positions within the same forest.

nodes.title: Surplus Killing vs. Specialist Predation (64 vs. 66)
nodes.description: The Mink (degree 64) introduced surplus killing -- killing more than needed in prey-dense environments. The Fisher introduces specialist predation -- a unique technique for killing armored prey (porcupines). Opposite predatory innovations from the same family: indiscriminate over-predation vs. precise specialized technique. Both novel behavioral categories for the engine.

nodes.title: Three of Four Reclassified -- Molecular Revolution in the Quartet
nodes.description: Otter: Lutra -> Lontra (1999). Mink: Mustela -> Neogale (~2000s). Fisher: Martes -> Pekania (~2008). Only the Marten retains Martes. Three of four quartet members were reclassified out of Old World genera by the same molecular phylogenetic revolution. 75% of the quartet's taxonomy was rewritten by molecular data. Pekania from Abenaki pekan -- a Native American-derived genus name.

nodes.title: Spotted Owl Conservation Connection (SPS-004)
nodes.description: Both the Fisher and the Northern Spotted Owl are dense-forest-dependent species with conservation stories. Both need closed canopy. Both are negatively affected by logging and fragmentation. In the Olympics and Cascades, territories overlap. The Owl became the poster species for old-growth conservation in the 1990s; the Fisher's extirpation and reintroduction is a newer chapter in the same story. Both produce rare nocturnal vocalizations from deep forest. 62 degrees apart, connected by forest dependency and conservation need.

nodes.title: Pileated Woodpecker Large-Tree Connection (SPS-054)
nodes.description: Both species need large trees in mature forest. The Pileated Woodpecker excavates cavities in large-diameter snags; Fishers den in natural cavities (and may use abandoned Pileated cavities). Both are limited by large-tree availability. Both associate with dense forest and structural complexity. Cavity architect and cavity tenant in the same forest.

nodes.title: Conservation Thread: Fur Trade to Extirpation to Return
nodes.description: Beaver (61-62): shaped by fur trade. Mink (64): shaped by fur trade AND farming, invasive when introduced. Fisher (66): ELIMINATED by fur trade and habitat loss, then RETURNED through managed reintroduction. The conservation thread intensifies across the mammalian era: commercial exploitation -> over-exploitation -> extirpation -> managed return. The Fisher is the fur trade story's most extreme expression -- a species trapped to local extinction and brought back by the same government agencies that once allowed its destruction.

nodes.title: The Neko Case Parallel
nodes.description: Both fierce, both with powerful vocalizations -- Case's volcanic voice parallels the Fisher's terrifying scream. Both wild and untameable -- Case resists categorization, the Fisher cannot be domesticated. Both reclaim abandoned territory -- Case in alt-country's margins, the Fisher reintroduced to forests from which it was extirpated. Case E=4, Fisher E=5 -- both deliver with maximum intensity. Both Tacoma-rooted and forest-adjacent.

nodes.title: Sixth Consecutive Original CSV Species
nodes.description: Degrees 61-66: six consecutive original CSV species. The longest original-CSV streak in engine history. Every mammalian entry has been an original CSV species. No substitutions needed or considered. The CSV's mammalian sequence is running clean and uncontested.

nodes.title: Seventh Mammalian Entry -- The Mammalian Era Deepens
nodes.description: Seven mammalian entries across six degrees (61-66). Five mammalian species: Beaver, River Otter, Mink, American Marten, Fisher. Two orders (Rodentia, Carnivora), three families (Castoridae, Mustelidae). Mammalian representation rises to 10.4% (7 of 67 entries). The engine was 100% avian for its first 60 degrees -- now over 10% mammalian. The mammalian era is six degrees deep and still running.

nodes.title: Fourth Carnivore -- All Mustelidae
nodes.description: Carnivora now has four entries, all from Mustelidae: Otter (63), Mink (64), Marten (65), Fisher (66). The order's entire representation comes from a single family. Carnivora quadrupled across the Mustelidae quartet. No non-mustelid carnivore has appeared. When a canid or felid eventually enters the engine, it will be the order's first non-mustelid representative.

nodes.title: The Misnomer -- A 'Fisher' That Doesn't Fish
nodes.description: The Fisher does NOT fish. It is not particularly associated with aquatic habitats. The name likely derives from French fiche (polecat pelt) or related European terminology, not from fishing behavior. This is the engine's most misleadingly named species -- a deep-forest predator called 'Fisher' that lives far from water. The misleading name contrasts with the habitat reality: the Fisher completes the quartet's DEPARTURE from water.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE MUSTELIDAE. Both arboreal forest predators. Marten = old-growth canopy, 0.5-1.2 kg. Fisher = dense forest, 2-6 kg. Fisher is 2-3x larger. The Marten scaled up and moved to the ground. Same forest, different scales and strategies. Canopy specialist vs. forest-floor generalist.

MUSTELIDAE QUARTET continues. Creek bank (Mink) vs. dense forest (Fisher). Both solitary predators. Both fierce relative to body size. Mink introduced surplus killing; Fisher introduces specialist predation (porcupine technique). Opposite predatory innovations from the same family.

QUARTET ENDPOINTS. River/estuary (Otter) vs. deep forest (Fisher). Social (Otter) vs. solitary (Fisher). The two largest mustelids in the engine (5-14 kg vs. 2-6 kg). The quartet's opening and closing species, representing the maximum ecological distance within Mustelidae.

DENSE-FOREST DEPENDENTS. Both need closed canopy and large trees. Both conservation stories (ESA listing vs. reintroduction). Both nocturnal. Both produce rare, emotionally disturbing vocalizations from deep forest. Habitat overlap in Olympics and Cascades. 62 degrees apart, connected by forest dependency.

LARGE-TREE DEPENDENCY. Both need large-diameter trees in mature forest. Pileated excavates cavities; Fisher dens in natural cavities. Cavity architect and cavity tenant. Both associate with dense forest and structural complexity.
Seventh mammalian entry. Fifth mammalian species. Fourth Carnivora, fourth Mustelidae. Mammalian representation rises to 10.4% (7 of 67 entries). Two orders, three families, five species.

Dense forest habitat. Closed-canopy specialist. Porcupine predator with trophic cascade (Fisher -> porcupine -> tree damage). Reintroduced species -- extirpated and returned through international conservation (BC to WA). Fur trade extirpation. Forest health indicator.
